summary: This guest post by a pre-settlement funding company CEO defends his industry while acknowledging its problems. Pre-settlement funding provides cash advances to plaintiffs in personal injury cases who can't wait years for settlements, with repayment contingent on winning. While the author argues this service helps level the playing field against insurance companies, he admits lack of regulation has allowed bad actors to charge predatory rates (35-40% per six months) without proper risk assessment. He proposes rate caps as a solution that would both protect consumers and force better underwriting, thereby limiting funding to meritorious cases only.
key_points: Pre-settlement funding is non-recourse: plaintiffs repay nothing if they lose, making it risky for funders but protective for borrowers
Insurance companies deliberately delay settlements to induce desperation and extract lowball offers from financially vulnerable plaintiffs
Most funding companies charge 35-40% per six-month period regardless of case risk, with additional broker and application fees
High rates allow companies to skip proper underwriting, which attorneys prefer because it's faster despite being worse for their clients
Rate caps would force better underwriting and limit funding to genuinely meritorious cases
money_quotes: Funding does more than ensure fair and complete compensation for the injured—it ensures that our justice system is blind to an individual's economic standing.
No matter how obvious the negligence or big the damages, insurance companies always delay compensation. It's the oldest and most effective trick in the book: gain leverage by inducing desperation.
Simply because a lack of regulation led to a swarm of bad actors doesn't mean pre-settlement funding should go away.
Believe it or not, attorneys whose clients bug them enough for cash will take a quicker funding process over lower rates every time.
They only realize the mistake when it comes time to repay the funder.
Rate caps will do more than stop unreasonable rates. They will also make it impossible for frivolous lawsuits to get funding and hurt our economy.
key_insight: Pre-settlement funding could promote justice system equity, but without rate caps, the industry's profit incentives create predatory lending disguised as plaintiff empowerment.
